title: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>, acme-procurement@acme.com
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Laura / Acme procurement team —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our discussion, I’m attaching a short proposal and a worked example of commit math so you can see how quarterly true-ups and make-goods would function under the committed tiers we outlined. High-level summary below: 

- Commit tiers (monthly equivalent): 1M tokens/mo, 5M tokens/mo, 20M tokens/mo. Tiered per-token discounts apply at each commit threshold.
- True-up cadence options: quarterly reconciliation (preferred) or annual reconciliation.
- Make-goods mechanics: if actual consumption < committed consumption over the reconciliation period, Redwoods issues a credit to match the committed revenue (make-good) or offers carry-forward to the next period by mutual agreement. Credits appear on the next invoice or can be settled as a standalone credit memo.

Questions to confirm from your side: 
1) Do you want quarterly true-ups (reconciles every calendar quarter) or a single annual true-up?
2) Any procurement constraints on credits vs. cash refunds? Some customers require credits, others accept pro-rated refunds — we can support both with 30–90 day timelines.

Attachments: Redwood-Hosted-Pricing-Terms-v2.pdf (proposal), commit_calculation_sheet.xlsx (worked math).

Next step: If quarterly works, we’ll send a short amendment that defines the commit amounts by quarter and the exact reconciliation flow. Vivek — I’m looping you for high-level approval on the discount stacks if Acme signs the 20M tier.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0118



From: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: acme-procurement@acme.com
Date: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Thanks Ben — this is helpful. A couple of clarifying questions from procurement before we move to an amendment: 

1) Stacking: if we commit to 20M tokens/mo, do we get both the commit-tier discount and any usage-tier discounts (e.g., overage volume discounts) or is it one or the other?
2) Make-goods: we prefer a credit memo rather than a cash refund. Can you confirm how credits show up on our account and whether they expire?
3) Timing: what is the invoice timing for the true-up? For example, if we under-consume in Q2, when will we see the credit applied?

Also: we will need a statement of work / amendment language that our legal team can review. Can you include a sample clause for the quarterly true-up and a sample cap on make-goods (we typically ask for a one-time cap on credits for the first year).

Appreciate the quick turnaround — Laura
Laura Kim
Head of Cloud Platform, Acme Corp
laura.kim@acme.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>, acme-procurement@acme.com
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Jun 2026 08:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Laura — great questions. Short answers first, then an example: 

1) Stacking: commit-tier discounts apply to the committed baseline. Overage usage (consumption > commit in a period) is billed at the overage rate, which can include additional volume discounts depending on monthly delta. In practice: the commit creates a guaranteed per-token price for the committed bucket; overages are billed per our published usage tiers unless we’ve included a negotiated overage cap in the amendment. 

2) Credits vs refunds: we can issue a credit memo applied to the next invoice by default. Credits issued for make-goods do not expire within 12 months; beyond that we’ll treat on a case-by-case basis. We can also support a cash refund for enterprise customers if procurement/legal requires it, but that will need approval from finance (Marissa is copied). 

3) Timing: for quarterly true-ups, reconciliation runs on the 10th business day after quarter close; credits (or invoice adjustments) appear on the following month’s invoice cycle. Example: Q2 closes 30 Jun, reconciliation completes mid-July, adjustment applied to Aug invoice. For annual true-up we follow the same pattern but on a fiscal-year basis.

Example (attached spreadsheet shows math):
- Committed: 5M tokens/mo (15M tokens/quarter) at $0.0008/token (committed price)
- Actual Q2 consumption: 12M tokens total.
- Shortfall: 3M tokens.
- Make-good: Redwood issues a credit equal to (3M tokens * committed price) = $2,400, unless you elect carry-forward where those 3M reduce next quarter’s committed requirement.

Make-good cap: we typically accept a negotiated cap on first-year credits (e.g., max 20% of annual committed value) — we’ll include a sample cap clause in the amendment draft.

Action items: Marissa, can you review the carry-forward vs credit memo language and confirm whether finance prefers credits only or if we can allow cash refunds subject to review? Vivek, if the customer commits to the 20M tier we will need sales leadership sign-off on a 10% onboarding discount; pinging you for approval path.

—Ben

On Fri, Jun 12, 2026 at 08:47 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> [attached: commit_calculation_sheet.xlsx]



From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 15 Jun 2026 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options (finance note)

Hi Laura / Ben —

I reviewed the proposed true-up flows and attached a short CSV with sample reconciliations for the first two quarters so you can see how credits post and how the GL entries would look. A few implementation notes from finance: 

- Credit memos: issued as account credits applied on the next invoice cycle. Default expiration 12 months.
- Cash refunds: we need a signed amendment and finance approval; typical lead time is 30–45 days after reconciliation completes.
- Carry-forward: allowed for one quarter only and must be elected at reconciliation time; carry-forward reduces the next quarter’s committed baseline (not stackable multiple quarters).
- Make-good cap: we can include a first-year cap of up to 20% of annual committed value. Any cap above that requires VP approval.

Attached: trueup_example_summary.csv. If this works, we will draft the amendment language (quarterly true-up + credit memo flow + first-year 20% cap).

Marissa Cole
Head of Finance Operations, Redwood Inference
marissa@redwood.com



From: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, acme-procurement@acme.com
Date: Wed, 05 Aug 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Ben / Marissa —

Thanks for the detail and the worked examples. Acme is comfortable moving forward with quarterly true-ups and the credit-memo make-good approach with the following conditions: 

- First-year make-good cap set to 15% of annual committed value (down from your proposed 20%).
- Credits must be reflected on the invoice following reconciliation (we accept 30-day settlement).
- We prefer credits over cash refunds unless finance requires otherwise.

Please prepare a short amendment with the quarterly true-up clause, the 15% cap, and the credit memo timeline. Also, Vivek — if you can confirm whether Redwood will offer a one-time 10% onboarding discount for the first three months, we’ll finalize approval on our side and route the amendment to legal.

Once we have the amendment we’ll turn it around quickly.

—Laura

On Wed, Aug 05, 2026 at 04:22 PM Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com> wrote:
> [accepted terms summary]
text:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>, acme-procurement@acme.com
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Laura / Acme procurement team —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our discussion, I’m attaching a short proposal and a worked example of commit math so you can see how quarterly true-ups and make-goods would function under the committed tiers we outlined. High-level summary below: 

- Commit tiers (monthly equivalent): 1M tokens/mo, 5M tokens/mo, 20M tokens/mo. Tiered per-token discounts apply at each commit threshold.
- True-up cadence options: quarterly reconciliation (preferred) or annual reconciliation.
- Make-goods mechanics: if actual consumption < committed consumption over the reconciliation period, Redwoods issues a credit to match the committed revenue (make-good) or offers carry-forward to the next period by mutual agreement. Credits appear on the next invoice or can be settled as a standalone credit memo.

Questions to confirm from your side: 
1) Do you want quarterly true-ups (reconciles every calendar quarter) or a single annual true-up?
2) Any procurement constraints on credits vs. cash refunds? Some customers require credits, others accept pro-rated refunds — we can support both with 30–90 day timelines.

Attachments: Redwood-Hosted-Pricing-Terms-v2.pdf (proposal), commit_calculation_sheet.xlsx (worked math).

Next step: If quarterly works, we’ll send a short amendment that defines the commit amounts by quarter and the exact reconciliation flow. Vivek — I’m looping you for high-level approval on the discount stacks if Acme signs the 20M tier.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0118



From: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: acme-procurement@acme.com
Date: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Thanks Ben — this is helpful. A couple of clarifying questions from procurement before we move to an amendment: 

1) Stacking: if we commit to 20M tokens/mo, do we get both the commit-tier discount and any usage-tier discounts (e.g., overage volume discounts) or is it one or the other?
2) Make-goods: we prefer a credit memo rather than a cash refund. Can you confirm how credits show up on our account and whether they expire?
3) Timing: what is the invoice timing for the true-up? For example, if we under-consume in Q2, when will we see the credit applied?

Also: we will need a statement of work / amendment language that our legal team can review. Can you include a sample clause for the quarterly true-up and a sample cap on make-goods (we typically ask for a one-time cap on credits for the first year).

Appreciate the quick turnaround — Laura
Laura Kim
Head of Cloud Platform, Acme Corp
laura.kim@acme.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>, acme-procurement@acme.com
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Jun 2026 08:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Laura — great questions. Short answers first, then an example: 

1) Stacking: commit-tier discounts apply to the committed baseline. Overage usage (consumption > commit in a period) is billed at the overage rate, which can include additional volume discounts depending on monthly delta. In practice: the commit creates a guaranteed per-token price for the committed bucket; overages are billed per our published usage tiers unless we’ve included a negotiated overage cap in the amendment. 

2) Credits vs refunds: we can issue a credit memo applied to the next invoice by default. Credits issued for make-goods do not expire within 12 months; beyond that we’ll treat on a case-by-case basis. We can also support a cash refund for enterprise customers if procurement/legal requires it, but that will need approval from finance (Marissa is copied). 

3) Timing: for quarterly true-ups, reconciliation runs on the 10th business day after quarter close; credits (or invoice adjustments) appear on the following month’s invoice cycle. Example: Q2 closes 30 Jun, reconciliation completes mid-July, adjustment applied to Aug invoice. For annual true-up we follow the same pattern but on a fiscal-year basis.

Example (attached spreadsheet shows math):
- Committed: 5M tokens/mo (15M tokens/quarter) at $0.0008/token (committed price)
- Actual Q2 consumption: 12M tokens total.
- Shortfall: 3M tokens.
- Make-good: Redwood issues a credit equal to (3M tokens * committed price) = $2,400, unless you elect carry-forward where those 3M reduce next quarter’s committed requirement.

Make-good cap: we typically accept a negotiated cap on first-year credits (e.g., max 20% of annual committed value) — we’ll include a sample cap clause in the amendment draft.

Action items: Marissa, can you review the carry-forward vs credit memo language and confirm whether finance prefers credits only or if we can allow cash refunds subject to review? Vivek, if the customer commits to the 20M tier we will need sales leadership sign-off on a 10% onboarding discount; pinging you for approval path.

—Ben

On Fri, Jun 12, 2026 at 08:47 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> [attached: commit_calculation_sheet.xlsx]



From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 15 Jun 2026 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options (finance note)

Hi Laura / Ben —

I reviewed the proposed true-up flows and attached a short CSV with sample reconciliations for the first two quarters so you can see how credits post and how the GL entries would look. A few implementation notes from finance: 

- Credit memos: issued as account credits applied on the next invoice cycle. Default expiration 12 months.
- Cash refunds: we need a signed amendment and finance approval; typical lead time is 30–45 days after reconciliation completes.
- Carry-forward: allowed for one quarter only and must be elected at reconciliation time; carry-forward reduces the next quarter’s committed baseline (not stackable multiple quarters).
- Make-good cap: we can include a first-year cap of up to 20% of annual committed value. Any cap above that requires VP approval.

Attached: trueup_example_summary.csv. If this works, we will draft the amendment language (quarterly true-up + credit memo flow + first-year 20% cap).

Marissa Cole
Head of Finance Operations, Redwood Inference
marissa@redwood.com



From: Laura Kim <laura.kim@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, acme-procurement@acme.com
Date: Wed, 05 Aug 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API: True-up action plan and make-good options

Ben / Marissa —

Thanks for the detail and the worked examples. Acme is comfortable moving forward with quarterly true-ups and the credit-memo make-good approach with the following conditions: 

- First-year make-good cap set to 15% of annual committed value (down from your proposed 20%).
- Credits must be reflected on the invoice following reconciliation (we accept 30-day settlement).
- We prefer credits over cash refunds unless finance requires otherwise.

Please prepare a short amendment with the quarterly true-up clause, the 15% cap, and the credit memo timeline. Also, Vivek — if you can confirm whether Redwood will offer a one-time 10% onboarding discount for the first three months, we’ll finalize approval on our side and route the amendment to legal.

Once we have the amendment we’ll turn it around quickly.

—Laura

> [accepted terms summary]

title: OrchardAI - Optimize toolkit savings & technical fit questions
preview:
From: Alec Turner <alec.turner@orchardai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Optimize toolkit questions + RFP

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking this — attaching our brief (OrchardAI_RFP_20260803.pdf). We're evaluating Redwood's Optimize toolkit to reduce per-token costs for our customer-facing summarization API. Two quick things we need clarity on before we commit:

1) How do batching and KV/prefix caching interact with our bursty traffic (mostly short summaries, p50 latency 120ms)? Any recommended slab/commit structures for that pattern?

2) Quantization guidance: we run a mix of 7B and 13B open models. Will the Optimize recommendations differ materially by model family? Are there compatibility or quality regressions we should budget for?

Would like to get an initial commercial sketch (credits vs committed slabs) and a short technical note on expected quality delta at different quant levels. Can we set a call to run through?

Best,
Alec Turner
Head of Infrastructure, OrchardAI
ale c.turner@orchardai.com

Attachment: OrchardAI_RFP_20260803.pdf (application/pdf, 410KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alec Turner <alec.turner@orchardai.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Optimize toolkit questions + RFP

Alec — thanks for the RFP and the clear ask. High level reply below, happy to deep-dive on a call this week.

Quick answers up front:
- Batching + KV caching: for short, bursty requests we recommend a cache-first approach with a small commit to preserve latency SLOs. That means configuring an aggressive prefix cache (TTL 60s for high-repeat queries) and a small batching window (8-12ms) to avoid added tail latency.
- Quantization: 7B tends to get Q4_0 safely; 13B often needs Q5_0 or mixed-precision to avoid measurable quality drift on summarization. We'll include our eval delta numbers in the follow-up note.

Commercial sketch: two paths we typically propose — Pilot Credits (short term, lower commit) or Slab Commit (better unit economics at scale). I'll attach a one-pager and cc Priyom for packaging and Karthik for the technical quant note.

Proposed next step: 30-minute call tomorrow between our teams to align on workload samples and target QPS so we can produce a short MAT and a formal proposal. Options: Tue 8/4 10am PT or Wed 8/5 9am PT. Which works?

Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0189

Attachment: redwood_optimize_sketch_20260804.pdf (application/pdf, 220KB)

From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Alec Turner <alec.turner@orchardai.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 13:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Packaging options + initial slate for MAT

Alec — following Ben's note, a couple of practical packaging options we see fit for OrchardAI's pattern: 

Option A — Pilot Credits (30–90d)
- 150k token credits prepaid, applies to Optimize Toolkit features (batching advisory, cache rules, quant guidance).
- No long-term commit; unit price ~1.8x hosted list but includes 1 technical tuning session and 2 eval runs.

Option B — Slab Commit (12 months)
- Commit bands: 0–500M, 500M–2B, 2B+ tokens per month with stepped discounts (15%, 25%, 35%).
- Includes quarterly quant evaluation (per-model), 24/7 ops escalation window, and discounted engineering time for integration.

Operational guardrails we recommend:
- Cache TTLs and eviction policies tuned to your repeat rate (we'll need a 7-day traffic sample).
- AB test quant levels on a representative prompt set (we can help build the prompt set and run automated evals).

If you prefer, we can build a short MAT (mutual action plan) that maps deliverables, timelines, and acceptance criteria — I'll draft that after the call.

— Priyom Das
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
priyom.das@redwood.com

Quoted text from previous message:
> Alec — thanks for taking this — attaching our brief (OrchardAI_RFP_20260803.pdf).

Attachment: packaging_options_orchardai_v1.pdf (application/pdf, 180KB)

From: Alec Turner <alec.turner@orchardai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Cc: Sofia Mendez <sofia.m@orchardai.com>
Date: Tue, 4 Aug 2026 08:21:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Optimize toolkit questions + RFP

Thanks Ben/Priyom — helpful start. A few clarifying items before we schedule:

1) On the slab commit discounts: do those discounts apply regardless of whether traffic is served hosted vs Dedicated? OrchardAI plans to use a hybrid model (hosted for public traffic, Dedicated for regulated enterprise customers).
2) For quantization: can you share a concrete example of expected ROUGE/ROUGE-L delta at Q4_0 vs Q5_0 for a 13B summarization model? We prefer a single-number summary we can present to our data science leadership.

Also — curious whether Redwood has any guidance for CPU fallback for lower-priority batches (we want predictable cost caps even when GPUs are constrained).

Can we do Wed 8/5 9am PT for a sync? That works for our side.

Thanks,
Alec

On Mon, Aug 3, 2026 at 11:02 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Alec — thanks for the RFP and the clear ask. High level reply below, happy to deep-dive on a call this week.

Attachment: Orchard_followup_questions.txt (text/plain, 2KB)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Alec Turner <alec.turner@orchardai.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 5 Aug 2026 09:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Quantization & batch-size guidance (brief)

Alec — quick technical note ahead of today's call. TL;DR: 7B -> Q4_0 recommended; 13B -> Q5_0 or Q4_1 hybrid depending on prompt sensitivity. Expected quality deltas (internal averages on our summarization benchmark):

- 7B: Q4_0 mean ROUGE-L delta ~ -0.6 pts vs FP16, latency down ~35% on supported kernels.
- 13B: Q5_0 mean ROUGE-L delta ~ -1.6 pts vs FP16; Q4_1 (mixed) ~ -0.9 pts. Latency improvements 25–40% with Q5_0 depending on sequence length.

Batching: for short summaries (avg tokens per request ~ 80) we recommend batching targets of 8–16 tokens-per-batch; larger batches add throughput but increase p95 by ~30–70ms.

CPU fallback: feasible for background/low-priority workloads using our Dedicated CPU lanes but expect a 6–10x token-cost increase vs GPU; good for asynchronous batch processing where latency is not critical.

We'll bring representative eval runs to the call and can run a quick AB test if you share ~500 prompts.

— Karthik Iyer
Applied ML / Inference Optimization, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.com

Quoted earlier:
> Priyom — following Ben's note, a couple of practical packaging options we see fit for OrchardAI's pattern:

Attachment: quant_shortnote_20260805.md (text/markdown, 8KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alec Turner <alec.turner@orchardai.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 5 Aug 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Optimize toolkit questions + RFP

Alec — great to get alignment on the call today. Recap and agreed next steps below: 

1) Pilot: OrchardAI will start with Option A (Pilot Credits) for 60 days to validate batch/cache settings and quantify quality at Q4_0 (7B) and Q5_0 (13B). Redwood will run 2 eval passes and supply a short report.
2) Data/sample: Please upload 500 prompt/response pairs to the Drive link and grant edit access to priyom.das@redwood.com and karthik.iyer@redwood.com by EOD Fri 8/6.
3) Commercial: We'll issue a pilot SOW and a draft slab commitment sheet for long-term economics. Ben to send commercial LOI and Priyom will include acceptance criteria in the MAT.

If that matches your read, I'll send the LOI and pilot SOW by tomorrow morning. Thanks again for the time.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0189

title: Clarifying procurement options for FinSecure — marketplace or enterprise agreement
preview:
From: Alicia Vega <alicia.vega@finsecure.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@finsecure.com, Maya Singh <maya.singh@finsecure.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: FinSecure RFP — clarification on marketplace vs enterprise purchase route

Hi Alex,

Thanks for taking our call last week. I've attached our RFP and a short pricing questionnaire — the procurement team is trying to map out the buying lanes and we need a clear summary from Redwood on whether we should pursue: (A) a marketplace private offer (AWS Marketplace / GCP Marketplace / Azure Private Offer) or (B) a direct enterprise procurement with an MSA and invoiced PO.

Specific questions we need addressed for our finance and security teams:
- Which route gives us the fastest procurement-to-production timeline for a reserved capacity pilot?
- Are there differences in seller-of-record and tax implications across marketplaces vs direct invoicing?
- Any constraints on CO-SELL credits or joint-sales eligibility if we go marketplace?
- How do subscription terms, termination, and data residency controls differ?
- Typical lead times for private-offer setup vs MSA negotiation at your end.

We attached the RFP and a short list of procurement questions. Can you respond with a brief pros/cons matrix and recommended path for a regulated fintech? We're hoping to get internal approval this month.

Thanks — happy to set a follow-up call once you've reviewed.

Best,
Alicia Vega
Head of Procurement | FinSecure
procurement@finsecure.com

Attachments: FinSecure_RFP.pdf; finsecure_pricing_questions.xlsx


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Vega <alicia.vega@finsecure.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2026 11:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FinSecure RFP — clarification on marketplace vs enterprise purchase route

Hi Alicia —

Thanks for sending the RFP and the questionnaire. I reviewed the attachments and pulled together an initial summary (attached) that highlights the main tradeoffs for a regulated fintech like FinSecure. High level: both routes can work; the right one depends on your procurement constraints and priority between speed and long-term billing/control.

Quick summary (pros/cons):
- Marketplace (private offer):
  * Pros: faster procurement turnaround (private offers can be issued in days once marketplace account setup is complete), predictable cloud-native billing, easier PO mapping in some finance systems.
  * Cons: seller-of-record depends on the marketplace and listing (AWS/GCP/Azure have different models), possible limitations on co-sell incentives unless we register the opportunity early, and sometimes less flexibility on contract language/termination terms.
- Direct enterprise contract (MSA + invoiced PO):
  * Pros: full control over legal terms, easier to negotiate SLAs, data residency language, and billing cadence (Net30/Net45). Co-sell and channel crediting is straightforward to document in our partner processes.

Subject: BCP/DR evidence request — RTO/RPO and runbook artifacts

Hi Camila,

- RTO & RPO targets by deployment type (hosted, dedicated, private) and any measured results from the last 12 months.
- A current DR runbook index (table of contents) and one example drill log.
- Evidence of warm-cache/k-v cache strategies for reducing cold-start RTO (screenshots or sample logs are fine).
- Recent sample audit logs that show failover & restore activity (anonymized).

Can you provide a single bundle or point me to where these artifacts live? Ideally by end of this week — our internal reviewers are on a tight schedule.

Tom Leiter

BlueBay Finance

From: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.com>

Tom —

Following up with the technical details you asked for. See the attached artifacts and the Drive folder linked below (we have a read-only collection for security reviews). Highlights:

- Hosted (regional failover tests): observed RTO (time from failover start to end-to-end inference validation): 95th pct = 5m 20s, 99th pct = 8m 45s (across three scheduled drills in us-west-2/us-east-1/eu-west-1; timestamps are in the attached runbook drill-log.csv with request IDs redacted).
- Dedicated: measured 95th pct RTO = 4m 10s, 99th pct = 6m 5s (note: dedicated pools have reserved capacity and automated priming).
- Private/on-prem: Redwood provides an optional warm-start agent that can be deployed by customers; for managed Private installs (contracted), Redwood can operate the warm-starts as part of an ops addendum. Operational responsibility is listed in the runbook_index_v2.xlsx under section 2.4.
- Warm-cache evidence: attached kv_warm_cache_screenshots.png (cache hit-rate graphs during a planned failover) and cloudtrail_sample.log (anonymized).

Drive (read-only): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcD_example_runbook
DocuSign (statement): https://app.docusign.com/BB-4352/evidence-statement

If you want a redacted CSV of the drill log in a specific schema, tell us the fields and we’ll produce it; otherwise the attached CSV contains timestamps, stage markers, and operator notes.

Regards,

Diego Fernandez
Director, Reliability Engineering | Redwood Inference
diego.fernandez@redwood.com

Attachments: kv_warm_cache_screenshots.png, cloudtrail_sample.log, runbook_drill-log.csv

title: MSA — Liability cap and damages exclusions
preview:
From: Olivia Grant <olivia.grant@acmeops.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sarah Li <sarah.li@acmeops.com>
Date: 2026-08-03T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: MSA - liability cap: proposal and questions

Camila —

Thanks for sending the MSA. Our legal team reviewed the draft and attached a redline (ACME_MSA_Redline.pdf). Two high-level items we need to resolve before procurement can move forward:

1) Liability cap: Our counsel prefers either a fixed cap of $5,000,000 or language that preserves carve-outs (IP indemnity, gross negligence, willful misconduct, and data breach) as unlimited. They also asked whether Redwood is open to an aggregate (total life of the agreement) cap vs. an annualized cap (per 12-month period).

2) Consequential damages: We see Redwood's standard language excludes consequential damages; counsel asked to confirm this covers indirect loss but not data-related direct remediation costs.

I've highlighted the relevant sections in the attached PDF. Happy to hop on a short call if that's easier — Sarah (cc'd) is ACME's procurement lead and will handle the commercial checklist once we have legal alignment.

Regards,
Olivia Grant
Senior Counsel, ACME Ops
olivia.grant@acmeops.com
+1 415 555 0142

From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Olivia Grant <olivia.grant@acmeops.com>
Cc: Sarah Li <sarah.li@acmeops.com>
Date: 2026-08-03T12:35:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MSA - liability cap: proposal and questions

Olivia —

Thanks for the redline and the clear summary. Appreciate you flagging the two items. High level from our side: Redwood's negotiation posture is to keep a commercially reasonable cap tied to fees paid to provide predictability for both parties; we typically propose the greater of $1,000,000 or 2x fees paid in the prior 12 months. That structure balances customer protection while keeping liability exposure aligned with usage.

On carve-outs, we are open to preserving IP indemnity and willful misconduct as exceptions to the cap. For data breaches we usually handle confidentiality/breach through indemnity and insurance obligations rather than an unlimited cap — happy to provide policy details.

I've attached a short counter-redline (Redwood_Counter_Redline.pdf) that implements the 2x/1M floor approach and preserves the standard carve-outs: IP indemnity, gross negligence/willful misconduct, and confidentiality/data processing obligations.

Would you be able to confirm whether ACME needs an aggregate lifetime cap or an annualized cap? If you prefer aggregate, we may need to discuss a higher multiple so the economics remain balanced.

If you like, I can loop in our Legal lead (Laura Bennett) and Jordan on the commercial side to finalize.

Best,
Camila Reyes
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila.reyes@redwood.ai
+1 650 555 0191

Attachment: Redwood_Counter_Redline.pdf

From: Olivia Grant <olivia.grant@acmeops.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sarah Li <sarah.li@acmeops.com>
Date: 2026-08-03T16:08:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MSA - liability cap: proposal and questions

Camila —

Thanks. I reviewed Redwood's counter. A few points from our counsel:

• $1M floor + 2x prior 12 months feels low for a mission-critical vendor; they pushed for either $5M or 3x annual fees.
• For security/data incidents, counsel wants those outside the cap (they view remediation and notification costs as direct damages).
• They also asked for an exception for IP indemnity and for liability arising from Redwood's storage/processing of ACME data.

Procurement (Sarah) also prefers an aggregate cap (life of the agreement) to reduce edge cases around timing of incidents. If Redwood is willing to move to 3x annual fees with a $2M floor for aggregate caps, that may be workable — but counsel will still seek carve-outs for data breach remediation.

Can you confirm Redwood's absolute position on data breach carve-outs and whether a 3x multiple with a $2M floor is something you can present to your leadership?

-Olivia

From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-04T09:06:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: MSA - liability cap: proposal and questions

Laura/Jordan —

Forwarding ACME's most recent comments. They pushed back on 2x/$1M and prefer aggregate caps; counsel is asking for carve-outs for data breach remediation and wants either $5M fixed or 3x annual fees with a $2M floor.

Key asks for legal:
- Can we accept 3x prior 12 months with a $2M floor if the cap is aggregate?
- Are we able to carve out data breach remediation costs (notification, forensics, credit monitoring) from the cap, or do we limit the carve-out to willful misconduct and IP?
- Any internal policy or insurance constraints we should flag (cyber limits, reinsurance)?

Jordan — if legal is OK with the compromise, would appreciate your quick read on whether Sales can accept $2M floor / 3x multiple for this account at ACME's current ARR commitment. Deal: ACME-3451.

Thanks — Camila

From: Olivia Grant <olivia.grant@acmeops.com>

From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-04T11:17:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Fwd: MSA - liability cap: proposal and questions

Camila/Jordan —

Thanks for the heads up and the context. Quick read: the customer's asks are reasonable commercially but there are tradeoffs. My recommended compromise and rationale below — this is what I would present back to ACME as a single-line counter to resolve quickly.

Recommended contract language (MSA, Section 12):
"Notwithstanding anything to the contrary, Supplier's aggregate liability to Customer for any and all claims arising out of or related to this Agreement shall not exceed the greater of (a) Two and a Half (2.5) times the aggregate fees paid by Customer to Supplier in the 12 months preceding the claim, or (b) Two Million U.S. Dollars (USD $2,000,000). Notwithstanding the foregoing, the cap shall not apply to (i) claims for infringement of third-party intellectual property rights for which Supplier is required to indemnify Customer, (ii) liability resulting from Supplier's gross negligence or willful misconduct, and (iii) liabilities arising out of Supplier's intentional or unlawful retention, disclosure or misuse of Customer Data. For clarity, direct costs reasonably necessary to respond to a verified data security incident (including forensic investigation, regulatory notification, and credit monitoring where required by applicable law) shall be treated as direct damages but shall be subject to the cap defined above unless otherwise required by applicable law."

Why this compromise:
- 2.5x gives a middle ground between 2x and 3x; it reduces upside exposure for Redwood while addressing ACME's concern for a higher multiple.
- $2M floor is materially higher than $1M and helps procurement justify the deal.
- We preserve IP and willful misconduct carve-outs. For data incidents, we propose that remediation costs are "direct damages" but remain within the cap — this balances ACME's need to cover remediation while keeping unlimited exposure off the table. If ACME insists on unlimited remediation costs carve-out, we should request confirmation of minimum cyber insurance limits and consider a pricing uplift.

Internal policy/insurance: our cyber primary limit is $10M; we can provide a certificate. If ACME wants remediation costs excluded from the cap entirely, need to escalate for underwriting and pricing (potentially a 5-10% uplift depending on account size).

If you want, I can draft a redline with the above language and a short negotiating note for Jordan to use when speaking with procurement. If Sales confirms they can accept the floor/multiple, I'll prepare the redline and we can route for signature.

-Laura Bennett
General Counsel, Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.ai

Attachment: none

Olivia —

Thanks for the detailed feedback and for outlining counsel's priorities. We discussed internally and would like to propose the following compromise to resolve the liability cap quickly:

Proposed compromise (summary):
• Cap: greater of (i) 2.5x aggregate fees paid in the prior 12 months, or (ii) $2,000,000 USD (aggregate cap for the life of the agreement).
• Carve-outs: IP indemnity and liability for gross negligence/willful misconduct remain outside the cap.
• Data incidents: direct remediation costs (forensic investigation, regulatory notification and required credit monitoring) will be treated as direct damages; however, they will remain subject to the aggregate cap above. If ACME requires unlimited remediation costs outside the cap, Redwood will need to escalate to underwriting and pricing and request evidence of minimum cyber insurance limits.

We believe this language balances ACME's need for meaningful protection and Redwood's need for predictable exposure. Laura has drafted the clause and is happy to include it as a redline if you want to proceed.

title: Private deployment — post-demo: egress playbook & PII/dataflow clarifications
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@meditrust.com>, Alex Chen <alex.chen@meditrust.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private deployment — post-demo: egress playbook & PII/dataflow clarifications

Hi Samantha / Alex —

Thanks again for taking the demo today. Great conversation; wanted to capture the follow-ups and the small set of technical clarifications your engineering team asked for so we can move the scope toward a scoped Private/VPC engagement. Action items below — I’ve tagged internal owners.

Summary from call:
- Goal: run Redwood Private inside your VPC (single-tenant control plane), process PHI-adjacent PII for triage flows, and ensure no unauthorized egress from your environment.
- Constraints: air-gapped test environment for initial pilots, strict 90-day retention on identifiable fields, and SIEM forwarding to your Splunk instance via outbound TLS to a fixed egress IP.

Key clarifications we need from you (please confirm / provide diagrams where possible):
1) Egress endpoints: you mentioned a short allowlist to Splunk and your private S3 for model sync. Can you confirm the final list and whether outbound to redwood-inference-hosted-artifacts is blocked?
2) Egress inspection: does your egress stack perform TLS interception (proxying with MITM cert)? This affects our runtime cert pinning and KMS/HSM flows.
3) Network topology for air-gapped pilot: will the pilot VMs have a NAT gateway or will you provide a controlled proxy jump-host?
4) Log retention & PII: you require 90-day retention for PII fields and an option to purge earlier on request. Which tokens/fields count as "identifiable" for your policy?

Immediate next steps (owners):
- Laura Bennett: draft technical checklist for VPC peering, subnets, and static egress IPs (Laura, can you include recommended firewall rules and minimum allowed ports?).
- Dev Patel: prepare a short note on how the Redwood runtime handles KV cache eviction and whether any keys/metadata are written to persistent logs in Private mode.
- Ben Carter: consolidate Meditrust responses and propose MLP (mutual launch plan) milestones for pilot.

Attachments: call recording, initial egress CSV (we populated a starting list during the call), and our Private network checklist (summary).

Samantha — if you can reply with the final egress allowlist and confirm TLS interception behavior, we’ll lock the next engineering sync for Thursday.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0189

---
Attached: 20260803-call-recording.mp4 (video), meditrust-proposed-egress-list.csv (text), redwood-private-network-checklist.pdf (pdf)


From: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@meditrust.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Chen <alex.chen@meditrust.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 17:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Private deployment — post-demo: egress playbook & PII/dataflow clarifications

Ben —

Thanks for the recap and helpful checklist. Quick answers inline: 

1) Egress endpoints:
- Splunk ingest: splunk-ingest.meditrust.net (outbound TCP 8088, TLS)
- Internal artifact S3 (VPC endpoint): s3-us-west-2.meditrust-internal (no public internet)
- We will NOT allow outbound to any third-party model hosting URLs.

2) TLS interception:
- We do not intercept TLS for traffic destined to external SaaS (no MITM). Internal egress passes through a corporate proxy for logging, but it preserves end-to-end TLS for destinations with trusted certs.

3) Air-gapped pilot topology:
- Pilot will live in a segmented subnet with a controlled egress NAT hosted in a DMZ. That NAT has two static IPs that we can provide.

4) PII/Retention:
- "Identifiable" per our policy includes name, email, SSN, and subscriber IDs. We can provide a field mapping CSV. Retention: 90 days default, ability to trigger manual purge via ticket (we need an API call pattern for this).

We’ll attach the network diagram and the field mapping file before Wednesday EOD. Can we sync Thursday at 10am PT for engineering?

— Samantha
Samantha Liu
Technical Program Manager, Security & Privacy | MediTrust Health
Samantha.Liu@meditrust.com

> On Mon, Aug 3, 2026 at 3:12 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Thanks again for taking the demo today. Great conversation; wanted to capture the follow-ups and the small set of technical clarifications...


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@meditrust.com>

Hi Samantha / Ben —

Thanks for the quick answers, Samantha. I took the liberty of drafting a recommended minimal egress rule-set and some notes specific to your constraints. Summary below; attaching a printable checklist.

Recommended minimal firewall/NAT rules (pilot subnet):
- Allow outbound TCP 443 to splunk-ingest.meditrust.net on static egress IPs (provided by Meditrust).
- Allow outbound connections to your internal S3 via VPC endpoint only; ensure no public S3 writes permitted.
- Block outbound to any *.redwood-hosted-models.example (we will rely on artifact sync to your internal bucket).
- Allow outbound to Redwood control-plane endpoints for license verification / update checks but only to whitelisted IP ranges (we can deliver a signed list).

Notes on TLS interception and client certs:
- If your proxy terminates TLS for outbound connections, we will need a path for our runtime to perform certificate pinning or to rely on a customer-provided CA in the runtime trust store. That has operational implications for KMS/RPC calls; Dev, can you prepare the two implementation options (CA import vs. mTLS) and trade-offs?

Logs & PII retention:
- In Redwood Private, application-level logs containing user text are configurable. By default we persist non-identifying telemetry for 30 days; identifying fields can be hashed or redacted before persistent storage. We can support an API-triggered purge for any retained payloads — Dev, estimate dev effort for exposed purge endpoint and audit trail.

Next engineering sync: Thursday 10:00am PT works for us. Agenda proposal: network diagram review, static egress IP exchange, purge API sketch, and pilot milestones (30/60/90 days).

Laura Bennett
Head of Solutions Engineering | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.com

Summary from call:
- Goal: run Redwood Private inside your VPC (single-tenant control plane), process PHI-adjacent PII for triage flows, and ensure no unauthorized egress from your environment.
- Constraints: air-gapped test environment for initial pilots, strict 90-day retention on identifiable fields, and SIEM forwarding to your Splunk instance via outbound TLS to a fixed egress IP.

Key clarifications we need from you (please confirm / provide diagrams where possible):
1) Egress endpoints: you mentioned a short allowlist to Splunk and your private S3 for model sync. Can you confirm the final list and whether outbound to redwood-inference-hosted-artifacts is blocked?
2) Egress inspection: does your egress stack perform TLS interception (proxying with MITM cert)? This affects our runtime cert pinning and KMS/HSM flows.
3) Network topology for air-gapped pilot: will the pilot VMs have a NAT gateway or will you provide a controlled proxy jump-host?
4) Log retention & PII: you require 90-day retention for PII fields and an option to purge earlier on request. Which tokens/fields count as "identifiable" for your policy?

Immediate next steps (owners):
- Laura Bennett: draft technical checklist for VPC peering, subnets, and static egress IPs (Laura, can you include recommended firewall rules and minimum allowed ports?).
- Dev Patel: prepare a short note on how the Redwood runtime handles KV cache eviction and whether any keys/metadata are written to persistent logs in Private mode.
- Ben Carter: consolidate Meditrust responses and propose MLP (mutual launch plan) milestones for pilot.

Attachments: call recording, initial egress CSV (we populated a starting list during the call), and our Private network checklist (summary).

Samantha — if you can reply with the final egress allowlist and confirm TLS interception behavior, we’ll lock the next engineering sync for Thursday.

Thanks,
Ben Carter

title: Post-demo: monthly spend band & procurement thresholds (Orchard Health)
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Cruz <samantha@orchardhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 2 Aug 2026 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo: monthly spend band & procurement thresholds (Orchard Health)

Hi Samantha — thanks again for the time today. Quick recap and the concrete asks we agreed on so your procurement team can evaluate: 

1) Monthly token volume bands (what you said on the call):
   - Pilot (MVP): 20M–50M tokens/month
   - Initial production: 100M–300M tokens/month
   - Scale target (12–18 months): 300M–800M tokens/month

2) Your procurement signal:
   - Procurement wants to know the expected monthly spend band and the threshold that triggers an approved PO vs. a contract/order form. You mentioned a PO threshold around $15k/month and legal review kicking in at ~$60k/month.

3) Unit economics we discussed (examples we committed to send formally):
   - Hosted API (curated LLM): example target unit price of $60 per 1M tokens for steady-state usage at the 100M+/mo band (volume-discounted).
   - Dedicated/reserved capacity: pricing moves to committed capacity model; comparable unit economics but with minimum monthly commit and separate infra fee.

Next steps (action items):
   - Samantha: confirm whether procurement requires a monthly PO threshold or an annual commitment to unlock faster procurement.
   - Sam/Procurement: confirm billing entity and whether they need us to accept a standard ORCH PO with net-30 or if they require contract + SOW first.
   - Redwood (Ben/Marissa): send a concise pricing summary and a 2-line SOW draft that maps to the pilot and to the production band.

Attaching the usage estimate sheet we filled on the call — helps illustrate unit pricing across bands. I’ll follow up with a short pricing one-pager from Marissa.

Thanks — happy to jump on a sync with procurement if that speeds things up.

— Aditya
Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya_rao@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0123

Attachment: Orchard-MAAP-Usage-Estimates.csv (CSV, 3 rows)


From: Samantha Cruz <samantha@orchardhealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Orchard Health Procurement <procurement@orchardhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 10:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: monthly spend band & procurement thresholds (Orchard Health)

Thanks for the summary, Aditya — helpful. A couple of clarifications from procurement side before we loop legal: 

- The PO threshold I mentioned is operationally true: POs under $15k/month can be handled by our platform team with a standard PO and net-30. Anything consistently above $15k/month needs a commercial contract + procurement review.
- For sustained spend > $60k/month we typically require an enterprise contract (standard terms) and 60-day lead time for billing setup.

On unit economics: the team is mostly trying to validate the $/1M token you shared. If your internal estimate for the 100M–300M band is $60 per 1M tokens, we need a simple table showing: list price, volume discount tiers, and any committed-capacity credits. That will determine whether we go hosted-first or aim for a Dedicated commitment.

Procurement attach: I’ve added our billing guidelines and vendor onboarding checklist. We’ll also need a W-9 and evidence of SOC 2 Type II for the vendor portal.

If you can send the pricing table and a short SOW (pilot + prod) today, I’ll share with procurement and try to get a preliminary answer about the PO vs contract path. If it helps, we can schedule a 30-min procurement sync on Thursday.

— Samantha
Samantha Cruz
Director of Platform Engineering | Orchard Health
samantha@orchardhealth.com

Attachment: (procurement@orchardhealth.com) vendor-guidelines.pdf (referenced)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Cruz <samantha@orchardhealth.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 4 Aug 2026 08:48:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: monthly spend band & procurement thresholds (Orchard Health)

Hi Samantha — attaching the pricing one-pager and a 1-page SOW draft. Below is a compact table you can paste into procurement: 

Redwood example pricing (net examples, illustrative):
- Tier A (pilot): 0–50M tokens/mo — list $150 per 1M; discounted $120 per 1M with a 3-month pilot credit
- Tier B (initial production): 50–300M tokens/mo — list $120 per 1M; committed 3mo+ volume discount to $80–$65 per 1M depending on exact band
- Tier C (scale): 300M–800M tokens/mo — committed capacity pricing; effective $60 per 1M at 500M+

Notes:
- Hosted API: flexible month-to-month billing; discounts apply with 3+ month commitments.
- Dedicated (Reserved): requires minimum 6–12 month commit; pricing materially better at scale but requires infra onboarding and SOW + invoice cadence.
- Billing/POs: we accept standard POs under $15k/mo; for ongoing monthly invoices above that threshold procurement review + an executed order form is required.

SOW draft maps pilot (4–8 weeks) and production milestone (enablement, baseline SLA). I attached: Redwood-pricing-guide-2026.pdf and SOW-draft-orchard-20260802.docx. Also included the W-9 and SOC 2 summary links in the one-pager.

If agreement looks fine to procurement, options:
  A) Start Hosted pilot under PO (if < $15k/mo) — fastest to go live.
  B) Contract + monthly invoices for > $15k/mo with a 30–60 day procurement lead.
  C) Reserved capacity (Dedicated) if you want predictable unit economics at >$60k/mo and are comfortable with 6–12 month commits.

Happy to join the procurement sync on Thursday. 

— Marissa
Marissa Cole
Principal Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
marissa_cole@redwood.com

Attachments: Redwood-pricing-guide-2026.pdf (6 pages), SOW-draft-orchard-20260802.docx (1 page), W-9.pdf

From: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Cruz <samantha@orchardhealth.com>, Orchard Health Procurement <procurement@orchardhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 4 Aug 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: monthly spend band & procurement thresholds (Orchard Health)

Great — thanks for the quick turnaround, team. One clarifying note to keep procurement aligned: based on Marissa’s table, if Orchard wants to start production at ~150M tokens/month, the expected run-rate at the conservative committed price would be ~150 * $80/1M = $12k/month (effective $80 per 1M). That sits under your $15k PO threshold so you could choose to start Hosted with a PO and avoid a full contract initially.

If Orchard expects to grow to ~400–500M tokens/month within a year and wants predictable pricing, the Dedicated reserved path (6–12 mo commit) gets you to ~ $60/1M effective and would cross the $60k/month procurement boundary that requires a contract and SLA.

Proposed next steps: 
- Samantha / Procurement: confirm whether you want us to proceed with a hosted pilot under a PO (fastest) or aim to start SOW+contract for a larger commit.
- If PO flow: procurement can email a PO to invoices@redwood.com and we’ll provision pilot access within 48 hours once PO is accepted.
- If contract flow: Marissa and I will introduce legal and send the order form + SOW for signatures.

I’ll block a 30-min slot on Thursday at 11am PT for the procurement sync unless you prefer a different time. 

Thanks — looking forward to next steps.

— Aditya
Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya_rao@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0123

title: Q3 Partner Program Tiering & Marketplace Fee Recalibration — AWS/Azure/GCP
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ramos <elena.ramos@amazon.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Q3 Partner Program Tiering & Marketplace Fee Recalibration — AWS/Azure/GCP

Hi Elena / AWS team,

Thanks for the heads up on the new marketplace fee bands you circulated last week. We reviewed the change and it materially impacts our unit economics on the hosted API (tokens/embeddings) and will push our per-query cost up ~9–12% on the mid-tail workload. Before we get pulled into escalations with Finance, I wanted to align internally and with you on negotiation levers.

High-level asks/asks from our side:

title: Data residency, subprocessors, and cross-border transfer — where requests execute
preview:
From: Sam Wright <sam.wright@bluemarblehealth.com>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Legal Team <legal@bluemarblehealth.com>
Date: 2026-08-02T09:12:00-04:00
Subject: Quick Q: where does my data get processed?

Hi Priyom,

We’re evaluating Redwood for two products and need to be explicit about execution locality before moving to procurement. Can you confirm:

- Which regions physically execute inference for the hosted API (default behavior)?
- Do requests ever transit other countries or subprocessors (cloud infra, logging, monitoring)?
- If we request EU-only processing, what guarantees and controls are available (DPA, region pinning, Dedicated/Private options)?

Also, our legal team would like a short list of subprocessors and whether any logs or artifacts are stored outside of the requested region. Tight timeline — appreciate a reply today if possible.

Thanks,
Sam
Sam Wright
Head of Platform, BlueMarble Health


From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sam Wright <sam.wright@bluemarblehealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-02T12:03:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Quick Q: where does my data get processed?

Hi Sam — thanks for the concise list. Short answers up front, followed by specifics and attachments.

Summary (high level):
- Hosted API default: execution typically runs in the nearest healthy region by routing policy; common endpoints are us-east-1 (Virginia) and eu-west-1 (Ireland).
- Transit & subprocessors: ephemeral request routing may cross regional egress/WAN hops inside our cloud providers; persistent artifacts (logs, metrics, audit trails) are stored according to account region and retention policy — by default they are retained in the US, unless EU pinning is requested.
- EU-only options: we offer region pinning at the account level (hosted), Dedicated reserved pools in a selected region, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments for strict locality. We can sign an EU DPA and provide SOC2 artifacts.

Details: see the attached residency diagram and subprocessor inventory for a map of typical paths. Key points to note:
1) Execution locality: model inference is performed on GPU instances in the selected region; we keep model weights resident in that region for pinned accounts. For general hosted accounts, we select the region by latency and capacity policy (it’s possible for failover routing to place execution in a different region).

2) Transient hops: network-level hops between cloud regions may appear in traceroutes (these are transit-level operations by cloud providers), but we do not persist request payloads during transit.

3) Logs & artifacts: application logs (errors, traces) and anonymized telemetry are stored for operational needs. By default those are retained in our primary analytics region (US). For customers requiring EU-only storage we offer:
   - Account-level EU residency (pinning) for hosted API (no persistent logs leave EU), or
   - Dedicated capacity provisioned in eu-west-1 with explicit subprocessor restrictions, or
   - Private deployment within customer VPC/on-prem where all disk and KMS keys are under customer control.

4) Subprocessors: listed in the attached CSV. Short list: AWS (eu-west-1 / us-east-1), GCP (europe-west1), Datadog (metrics), Sentry (error aggregates), Cloudflare (CDN). Each entry in the inventory includes processing purpose and region.

5) Legal & export control considerations: where customers require it, we can add contractual commitments (DPA, SCCs/UK Addendum, EU data processing addendum) and support region-specific KMS scoping.

If you want, Ben can add a note about what exactly we keep in logs and retention windows.

Best,
Priyom
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>, Sam Wright <sam.wright@bluemarblehealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-03T08:17:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Quick Q: where does my data get processed? — technical notes and artifacts

On Wed, Aug 2, 2026 at 12:03 PM Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Summary (high level):
> - Hosted API default: execution typically runs in the nearest healthy region by routing policy; common endpoints are us-east-1 (Virginia) and eu-west-1 (Ireland).

Adding technical detail about what we persist and where — for BlueMarble’s review:
- Payload handling: request payloads (input text) are held in-memory in worker processes on the GPU host for the duration of the request. We do not write request payloads to disk as part of normal inference.
- Caches: KV cache/prefix caches are ephemeral and bound to the serving instance; we expire caches per-account TTLs (configurable).
- Audit logs & metrics: minimal request metadata (timestamp, model, token counts, anonymized status codes) is stored for billing and SLO purposes. Those records are sharded by region but, unless you opt into EU residency, are consolidated to our primary analytics cluster (US) for aggregation.
- Backups & snapshots: model artifacts and backups are stored in region-specific object stores; for pinned accounts we ensure storage buckets are in the pinned region.

Legal ports (operational note): we sometimes route cross-border control-plane traffic through designated "legal port" endpoints (e.g., Ireland and Virginia) to centralize compliance checks and DPA triggers. These are not storage endpoints; they are control-plane hops for orchestration (auth, billing metadata).

Attachments included: residency-diagram.svg (visual map), subprocessor-inventory.csv (detailed roster), redwood-soc2-extract.pdf (redacted SOC2 extract).

If BlueMarble requires we can produce a compact evidence pack that maps each subprocessor to the specific data types they might touch.

—Ben
Principal Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference


From: Sam Wright <sam.wright@bluemarblehealth.com>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Legal Team <legal@bluemarblehealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-03T13:02:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Quick Q: where does my data get processed? — follow ups

Thanks Priyom and Ben, that’s helpful. A couple clarifying asks from legal: 

1) For EU-only pinning, will you sign an EU Data Processing Addendum and confirm no persistent logs leave EU regions?
2) For hosted fallbacks (failover to other regions), can you provide an explicit list of circumstances that trigger cross-region execution?
3) Do any subprocessors (monitoring, error reporting) ever receive raw request content? If so, how is that data redacted/limited?

Also we’d like to schedule a short 30-minute call to review the attachments and have your security lead walk our engineer through the evidence pack. Are you available tomorrow morning (EU time)?

Sam


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sam Wright <sam.wright@bluemarblehealth.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-04T17:45:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Quick Q: where does my data get processed? — commitments and next steps

Hi Sam — thanks for the questions; answering in order and proposing next steps.

1) EU DPA & logs: Yes — we will execute an EU Data Processing Addendum for accounts that request EU residency. For EU-pinned hosted accounts we commit that persistent logs, billing records, and retained telemetry are stored only in EU region storage. Ephemeral metrics used for global SLOs are anonymized before aggregation.

2) Failover triggers: cross-region execution can occur in the following documented circumstances:
   - Planned maintenance or when regional capacity is exhausted and customer has not purchased Dedicated capacity in their preferred region,
   - Severe regional outage where cross-region fallback is necessary to preserve availability (we document failover in our runbooks and notify customers),
   - Customer explicitly opts into cross-region routing for latency reasons.
We will provide a short table mapping each trigger to the expected notification/mitigation workflow.

3) Subprocessor handling of content: no subprocessor should receive raw request payloads unless a customer explicitly enables forwarding for debugging or we are fulfilling a legal obligation. Monitoring and error aggregates receive sanitized/error-only payloads; Sentry/Datadog receive hashed/stripped identifiers and exception traces. We can include the redaction policy in the evidence pack.

Next steps (recommended):
- I’ll send a DocuSign link for our standard EU DPA and a checklist for EU pinning (account change + billing impact).
- Schedule a 30-min technical walkthrough with our security engineer — available slots: 2026-08-05 09:00–11:00 CEST or 2026-08-06 08:00–10:00 CEST.
- We’ll prepare a scoped evidence pack tying each subprocessor to the exact data types and retention windows.

Links: DPA draft (DocuSign) — https://www.docusign.com/example-dpa-link
Evidence folder — https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/0B-example-docs-policy

If that works I’ll loop in our security lead and get the DPA over today.

Best,
Laura Bennett
Head of Revenue Operations, Redwood Inference

From: Sarah Liu <sarah.liu@datahealth.co>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>, Grace Oconnor <grace_oconnor@redwood.ai>
Cc: Legal Team <legal@datahealth.co>
Date: 2026-08-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Temporary replication window for analytics workload

Hi Aisha / Grace,

Quick one — our analytics team needs a short-lived replica of EU customer traffic in a US analytics cluster for a one-off model training run next week. DataHealth must keep any cross-border copies for the shortest practical period; performance for the training job requires local compute in the US for a 48–72 hour window.

Can Redwood provide a documented runbook for:
- the exact retention window for temporary replicas,
- encryption at rest and key controls,
- subprocessors that would process the replicated data, and
- an audit trail we can hand to our privacy team?

We're aiming for the model training on Aug 6–7; please let me know what evidence you can provide and any formal signoffs you need.

Thanks,
Sarah
Sarah Liu
Head of Data Platforms | DataHealth

Attachment: training-job-schedule.xlsx (attachment stub)

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Sarah Liu <sarah.liu@datahealth.co>
Cc: Grace Oconnor <grace_oconnor@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-01T11:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Temporary replication window for analytics workload

Sarah — thanks for the heads up. We can support a short-lived replication for analytics with a few commitments, but we need to get the operational and legal knobs lined up. High-level proposal from our side: 

- Temporary replica window: up to 7 days maximum, with default safe option at 72 hours for the training job you described.
- Retention enforcement: automated TTL at the storage layer + forced cache/kv eviction after TTL.
- Encryption: replica will use the same KMS keys as your region; we can restrict key access to a limited operator role.
- Subprocessors: full subprocessors list + planned transient subprocessor disclosures (we'll include names for any cloud provider services used).
- Audit: we can emit an audit bundle (access logs, replica creation + deletion events) suitable for your privacy team.

I'll loop in Ben for the ops details and Marissa for contract-side language we should put into an addendum. I attached a light runbook draft to kick off this conversation.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Enterprise Sales Manager | Redwood Inference

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>

Date: 2026-08-02T08:50:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Temporary replication window for analytics workload

On Mon, Aug 02, 2026 at 08:50 Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai> wrote:

Aisha / Sarah — quick ops note and constraints: 

- We can spin up a region-bound replica (US-east) that will be created from snapshots of the EU store. Creation time for a normal dataset of your size is ~45–90 minutes.
- We recommend a default TTL of 72 hours for the replica to match your ask; we can extend to 120 hours with approval, but anything >72 hours requires a written exception and added logging retention.
- Replica lifecycle: snapshot -> replica instance -> ingestion -> model run -> verified destroy. The destroy process is synchronous and will emit deletion confirmation to the audit bundle.
- KMS: we can use customer-managed keys (bring-your-own-key) in the US region. If you use Redwood-managed keys, access is limited to a small operator principal; all access is logged.
- Network egress: we'll route via our private peering where available; otherwise standard TLS over public egress with routing metadata captured for the audit.

Operational asks for you: confirm dataset size, the exact window you want (start/end datetimes) and whether you require an escrow of the audit bundle.

Ben

What we’ll demo (short live demo + slides):
- Live routing scenario: request-level routing between standard and quantized model variants, and automated fallback using cache + prefix hits.
- A small benchmark: latency, p95, and cost delta for 1k QPS simulated across 256 token avg length.

title: Integration rehearsal: temporary runway & autoscale exception request
preview:
From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Integration rehearsal: temporary runway & autoscale exception request

Hi team,

Quick summary: Orion Health is planning a multi-week integration rehearsal (end-to-end traffic with production-like payloads) to validate cold-start behavior, cache warming, and billing events ahead of their public launch. They want to run two sustained weekends of high-concurrency tests starting the weekend of Aug 14.

Why we need an exception:
- Expected steady-state QPS: 1,200 reqs/s during peak windows (short bursts to 2,000).
- Model: r-infer-13B (FP16) with KV cache; warm-up time for cold starts is ~18s without prefix cache.
- Primary concern: cold-start tail latency causing false-positive SLA alerts and noisy replay for their telemetry.

Ask:
1) Ephemeral reservation: 12 GPU-equivalents (A100-class) reserved for Aug 14-16 and Aug 21-23 (weekend windows) with no preemption during the test windows (08:00-22:00 PDT).
2) Autoscale soft-limit increase: allow burst up to 16 GPUs for 30m windows during the scheduled tests to absorb spikes.
3) Billing: charge to DEAL-7193 cost center; product will absorb overage for the two rehearsal weekends.

Trade-offs we can accept:
- Preemptible outside the test windows (22:01-07:59).
- Lower priority than enterprise SLA customers; will fall back if capacity is exhausted outside the reserved windows.

Attachments: integration-runbook.pdf (runbook + rollback steps), capacity-forecast-aug.csv (detailed expected token volume).

Next step: Marcus or infra team, can you confirm feasibility and conflict with other reservations? Kimberly/Tessa, if ops can sign off, I will lock the dates with Orion.

Thanks,
Laura
--
Laura Bennett
Senior PM, Dedicated Capacity Programs
Redwood Inference

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-01T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Integration rehearsal: temporary runway & autoscale exception request

On Sun, Aug 1, 2026 at 9:12 AM Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Quick summary: Orion Health is planning a multi-week integration rehearsal...

Thanks for the detail, Laura. Short answer: possible, but a few operational constraints and questions before we can commit to non-preemptible reservations at that scale.

Operational notes / concerns:
- US-WEST1 (the requested region) has two overlapping pilots (Brightline and Nimbus) the week prior; 12 A100-equivalents blocks a sizable chunk of our headroom.
- Autoscale bursts to 16 are feasible, but we need a guardrail: maximum sustained burst duration per hour cannot exceed 45m without impacting other commitments.
- We'd need the runbook to include an automated rollback step that reclaims the reservation if cold-start tail persists beyond target SLA (to avoid noisy alarms).

Alternatives to reduce footprint:
- Shorter token contexts via prefix trimming (Orion agreed to try this in earlier tests).
- Use a 27B quantized variant that drops per-request GPU time by ~25% (tradeoff: slightly worse quality).
- Schedule heavy replay during off-peak night windows to shift load.

Please send the runbook link and a single-sheet business justification so I can pre-authorize with capacity planning. If Kimberly approves prioritization trade (time-of-day handback), I can tentatively reserve the slice and flag it as \"ephemeral-runway-ORION-7193\".

- Marcus
Principal Infra Ops

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-01T13:21:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Integration rehearsal: temporary runway & autoscale exception request

Thanks — I support giving Orion a runway for the rehearsal provided we attach strict conditions. My approval in principle with the following must-haves:

1) Time-boxed windows only (exact start/end times per weekend).
2) Auto-reclaim: if utilization is < 30% for a continuous 60-minute window during the midday block, infra reclaims capacity automatically.
3) Telemetry hooks: product team must stream token-level latency histograms to the Console and tag the rehearsal with DEAL-7193.
4) Cost cap: limit spending to $XXk per weekend (Finance will sign off once we have the single-sheet).

If Marcus can implement the auto-reclaim and we get the single-sheet business justification by EOD Aug 6, Kimberly approves the reservation.

Also: Laura, please confirm whether Orion will accept a pre-warmed cache artifact (we'll provide a KV snapshot) — that would reduce the GPU runway needed by ~25%.

- Kimberly
Director, Product Ops

From: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-02T09:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Integration rehearsal: temporary runway & autoscale exception request

Kimberly — thanks for the guidance. I want to lock this down today so Orion can finalize their test plan. Summary of asks against Kimberly's conditions:
- Runbook (attached) includes auto-reclaim implementation and exact window boundaries (we already added reclaimer hook).
- Orion agrees to the telemetry hooks and cost cap; finance contact is cc'd on their side.
- Orion will try the KV snapshot approach; if warm artifacts reduce needed GPUs we will scale down the reservation to 9 GPUs and keep burst to 12.

Please confirm (explicit \"approve\") and I'll create the reservation request into procurement with costs charged to DEAL-7193. Marcus, if you can provision a tentative ephemeral slice named ephemeral-runway-ORION-7193 and a reserve ticket in INFRA-8421, we'll tie the Jira ticket to the runbook and HubSpot record.

Regards,
Aditya
Head of Customer Integrations
Redwood Inference

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-03T15:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Integration rehearsal: temporary runway & autoscale exception request

Approved and actioned per Kimberly's conditions.

What I did:
- Created ephemeral reservation: ephemeral-runway-ORION-7193 (initial size 12 GPUs) in us-west1.
- Configured autoscale soft-limit to 16 GPUs with per-hour burst cap of 45m; auto-reclaim rule deployed (INFRA-8421).
- Linked runbook and Jira: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1A2b3C_example-runbook and JIRA: INFRA-8421.
- Added billing tag DEAL-7193 to the reservation.

Notes and next steps:
- If Orion accepts the KV snapshot and we observe steady-state utilization < 70% during the dry-run on Aug 10, we'll reduce the slice to 9 GPUs and lower burst limit to 12.
- Marcus will monitor and post a daily digest to the Console channel during the rehearsal weekends.

Marcus
Principal Infra Ops
Redwood Inference
